ses_18e0c8a75ffe7CjWP4LeT9uOPq.objective: Use TypeScript. Build it as a tiny MCP tool server whose only job is: receive an image path/URL + prompt, send both to a configurable vision model through OpenRouter, return the vision model's analysis to non-vision MCP clients. MCP servers expose capabilities through tools, resources, and prompts. For this project, a tool is enough. Tools are the MCP primitive intended for external actions/API calls. ([Model Context Protocol][1]) Architecture: ```text Non-Vision Model │ ▼ MCP Client │ ▼ Vision MCP Server │ ├── Local file loader ├── URL image loader ├── Base64 encoder │ ▼ OpenRouter Vision Model │ ▼ Structured result │ ▼ Non-Vision Model ``` Project structure: ```text vision-mcp/ ├── src/ │ ├── server.ts │ ├── config.ts │ ├── openrouter.ts │ ├── image.ts │ ├── tools/ │ │ └── analyzeImage.ts │ └── types.ts ├── package.json ├── tsconfig.json ├── README.md └── .env.example ``` MCP capability design: Tool name: ```text analyze_image ``` Input schema: ```json { type : object , properties : { prompt : { type : string }, image : { type : string , description : Local file path or URL } }, required : ["prompt", image ] } ``` Output: ```json { analysis : ... } ``` Server config: Environment variables: ```env OPENROUTER_API_KEY= OPENROUTER_MODEL=google/gemma-4-31b-it:free ``` Optional: ```env OPENROUTER_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1 MAX_IMAGE_MB=20 ``` MCP config example (Claude Desktop style): { mcpServers : { vision : { command : node , args : [ /absolute/path/vision-mcp/dist/server.js ], env : { OPENROUTER_API_KEY : sk-or-xxxx , OPENROUTER_MODEL : google/gemma-4-31b-it:free } } } } Implementation steps for Codex: 1. Create MCP server with TypeScript SDK. 2. Register a tool called `analyze_image`. 3. Detect whether `image` is: * local file path * http/https URL 4. If local: * read file * determine MIME type * base64 encode 5. Convert image into OpenAI/OpenRouter multimodal message format. 6. Send request to configured OpenRouter model. 7. Return response text through MCP tool result. 8. Add robust error handling. 9. Support stdio transport first. 10. Add optional Streamable HTTP transport later. MCP server skeleton: ```ts const server = new Server( { name: vision-mcp , version: 1.0.0 }, { capabilities: { tools: {} } } ) ``` MCP commonly uses JSON-RPC and stdio transport. Clients are expected to support stdio, making it the simplest deployment target. ([Model Context Protocol][2]) Tool registration concept: ```ts server.tool( analyze_image , { prompt: z.string(), image: z.string() }, async ({ prompt, image }) => { const result = await analyze(prompt, image) return { content: [ { type: text , text: result } ] } } ) ``` OpenRouter request format: ```json { model : google/gemma-4-31b-it:free , messages : [ { role : user , content : [ { type : text , text : Describe this image }, { type : image_url , image_url : { url : data:image/png;base64,... } } ] } ] } ``` Image handling plan: Local image: ```text C:\images\cat.png ``` ↓ ```text readFile ``` ↓ ```text data:image/png;base64,... ``` ↓ ```text OpenRouter ``` Remote image: ```text https://example.com/cat.png ``` ↓ ```text pass directly as image_url ``` ↓ ```text OpenRouter ``` Useful tool examples for users: Caption image: ```json { prompt : Describe this image in detail , image : ./cat.png } ``` OCR: ```json { prompt : Extract all visible text , image : ./invoice.jpg } ``` UI analysis: ```json { prompt : Explain this UI and identify bugs , image : ./screenshot.png } ``` Code screenshot: ```json { prompt : Read this code and explain it , image : ./code.png } ``` Future tools: ```text analyze_image ocr_image describe_ui extract_table compare_images ``` For v1, keep only: ```text analyze_image ``` Resources and prompts are unnecessary initially because the server is acting as a vision proxy. MCP resources are more appropriate for exposing reusable data/files, while prompts are reusable templates. The core value here is a callable tool. ([Model Context Protocol][3]) Important security requirements: * Restrict local file access to allowed directories. * Validate MIME types. * Enforce image size limits. * Sanitize paths. * Timeout OpenRouter requests. * Never log API keys. * Add rate limiting. * Avoid arbitrary command execution through stdio transports. Recent MCP ecosystem security research highlights risks around unsafe execution patterns. ([Tom's Hardware][4]) Docs Codex should read before implementation: * [MCP Specification Overview](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/basic?utm_source=chatgpt.com) * [MCP Tools Specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/server/tools?utm_source=chatgpt.com) * [MCP Transport Specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/basic/transports?utm_source=chatgpt.com) * [MCP Python SDK Examples (concepts transferable to TS)](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/python-sdk?utm_source=chatgpt.com) * [MCP Example Servers](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/examples?utm_source=chatgpt.com) Final v1 goal: ```text Any non-vision model + analyze_image MCP tool + OpenRouter vision model = vision capability added externally without changing the model itself ``` Sources: MCP exposes tools/resources/prompts and uses JSON-RPC over stdio/HTTP transports. Tools are the correct abstraction for invoking external services such as OpenRouter. ([Model Context Protocol][1]) [1]: https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/server/tools?utm_source=chatgpt.com Tools [2]: https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-03-26/basic/transports?utm_source=chatgpt.com Transports [3]: https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/server/resources?utm_source=chatgpt.com Resources [4]: https://www.tomshardware.com/tech-industry/artificial-intelligence/anthropics-model-context-protocol-has-critical-security-flaw-exposed?utm_source=chatgpt.com Anthropic's Model Context Protocol includes a critical remote code execution vulnerability - newly discovered exploit puts 200,000 AI servers at risk

ses_18e0c27d9ffe619rzLhZIOqEuC.objective: I'd avoid `desktop` because it sounds like remote-control software. Better names: * `screen` ← best * `window` * `viewport` * `observe` * `snapshot` * `capture` * `workspace` * `display` I'd pick: ```text vision screen ``` Very obvious to both humans and models. --- ### Tool 1: vision Purpose: ```text Give image understanding to non-vision models. ``` Input: ```json { prompt : Describe what's wrong , image : path/url/base64 } ``` Output: ```json { analysis : ... } ``` Backed by OpenRouter. --- ### Tool 2: screen Purpose: ```text Expose screenshots of applications/windows. ``` --- ## MVP Scope Only support: ```json { action : capture , target : wezterm } ``` or ```json { action : capture , pid : 1234 } ``` Nothing else. No clicking. No keyboard. No automation. Only screenshots. --- ## Internal Architecture ```text screen │ ├── Window Resolver ├── Platform Backend └── Image Exporter ``` --- ## Step 1: Resolve Target Input: ```json { target : wezterm } ``` Find matching process. Output: ```json { pid : 1234, title : WezTerm } ``` Resolution order: ```text PID ↓ Exact window title ↓ Process name ↓ Partial match ``` --- ## Step 2: Locate Window Create common interface: ```ts interface WindowInfo { id: string pid: number title: string } ``` Platform-specific implementations: ```text windows.ts linux.ts macos.ts ``` --- ## Step 3: Capture Window Common interface: ```ts interface ScreenProvider { captureWindow(window: WindowInfo): Promise<Buffer> } ``` --- ### Windows Use: ```text EnumWindows GetWindowThreadProcessId PrintWindow ``` Flow: ```text Find HWND ↓ Capture HWND ↓ PNG Buffer ``` PrintWindow works better than monitor screenshots. --- ### macOS Use: ```text CGWindowListCopyWindowInfo CGWindowListCreateImage ``` Flow: ```text Find window ↓ Capture window ↓ PNG ``` --- ### Linux Detect: ```text X11 Wayland ``` #### X11 Use: ```text XGetImage ``` or ```text xwd ``` #### Wayland Use: ```text xdg-desktop-portal ``` Important: ```text Wayland may require user permission. ``` Document this clearly. --- ## Step 4: Export Image Store: ```text temp/ ├── capture_1.png ├── capture_2.png ``` Return: ```json { image : /tmp/capture_1.png , width : 1920, height : 1080 } ``` --- ## Step 5: Integrate With Vision Tool Agent flow: ```text screen ↓ image path ↓ vision ↓ analysis ``` Example: ```json { action : capture , target : wezterm } ``` returns ```json { image : /tmp/wezterm.png } ``` then ```json { prompt : Read the terminal output and identify the error. , image : /tmp/wezterm.png } ``` --- ## Future Extensions (Same Tool) Without adding new MCP tools: ```json { action : capture_active } ``` ```json { action : capture_all } ``` ```json { action : capture_region , x : 0, y : 0, width : 500, height : 300 } ``` Because they're all screenshot operations, they belong under `screen`. --- Final MCP surface: ```text vision screen ``` Everything else is implementation detail. A model can inspect any application by chaining: ```text screen → vision ``` which is exactly the minimum capability needed for self-debugging UIs.
ses_18e0c27d9ffe619rzLhZIOqEuC.completionSummary: Added `screen` MCP tool for window capture on Windows. Uses PowerShell with inline C# (Win32 P/Invoke: EnumWindows, PrintWindow) to find and capture application windows by PID or process name. Saves PNG screenshots to temp dir, returns image path + dimensions. Full integration test verified: tools/list returns both tools, screen capture by name (Code) and PID (WindowsTerminal) both return 1920x1080 captures, missing args returns proper MCP error. 6 source files, all under 140 lines each, clean build.
